worth_it_explanation: Connected TV (CTV) buying is often evaluated on device-level impressions, which count the household as the unit and miss every additional viewer in the room. Samba TV's co-viewing data exists specifically to correct that gap. These checks confirm whether the team is applying it, whether co-viewing-adjusted figures are kept separate from the device-level and cookie-level digital metrics they sit next to in cross-screen reports, and whether any cross-platform comparisons are noted as being on the same measurement basis. The fixes are reporting discipline changes, not platform rebuilds.
intro: Samba TV measures audiences through Automatic Content Recognition (ACR) data collected from opt-in smart TVs. Because a single television is regularly watched by more than one person, Samba TV produces co-viewing estimates that translate household-level device signals into person-level reach and impression figures. These checks audit whether co-viewing measurement is being used and whether co-viewing-adjusted CTV figures are kept methodologically consistent with the other media metrics they are compared against in cross-screen reporting.

audit_checks.title: CTV reach and impressions are read at the device level rather than with co-viewing applied
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Reading CTV reach at the device level systematically understates how many people an ad reached. The campaign appears less efficient on a cost-per-person basis than it actually is, which can lead to underinvestment in CTV or to budget shifts away from a channel that is working better than the device-level numbers suggest.

educate.base:
A connected TV (CTV) device registers as a single household unit. When a campaign reports impressions and reach using device-level counts, each ad exposure is credited to one household regardless of how many people were in the room when the ad played. The total person-level audience reached is higher than the device count, sometimes by a meaningful margin since televisions are shared screens.

Samba TV's Automatic Content Recognition (ACR) data comes from opt-in smart TVs and captures what content is on the screen. Samba TV layers co-viewing data on top of that signal to estimate how many people are in the household at a given time, translating the household-level device signal into a person-level audience estimate. When co-viewing is applied, the same campaign that registered a certain number of device impressions produces a higher person-level reach figure and a different cost-per-thousand-persons (CPMP) than the raw device CPM suggests.

The practical effect of not applying co-viewing is that CTV looks less efficient than it is when compared against digital formats that are measured at the individual level. A display or mobile campaign reaches one person per ad impression by design; a CTV impression at the household level does not reflect the same unit. Mixing device-level CTV figures with person-level digital figures makes CTV appear to reach fewer people at a higher cost, which can distort channel allocation decisions.

A few signs this check applies:
  • The Samba TV report shows device-level reach or device impressions as the primary reach figure, with no co-viewing-adjusted column present in the report view being used for planning or optimization
  • CTV reach is being compared directly against digital display or social reach figures without a note that the measurement bases differ
  • Campaign performance summaries cite Samba TV reach numbers without clarifying whether those figures are household (device) or person-level
You may want to confirm which reach column in the Samba TV report the team is pulling into its performance summary before drawing cross-channel conclusions.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer running CTV alongside display and paid social, person-level reach figures from Samba TV's co-viewing estimates give a more direct comparison to the individual-level reach figures that display and social channels report. You may want to confirm the Samba TV report view being used in cross-channel summaries is the co-viewing-adjusted view rather than the raw device reach column.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser using Samba TV to measure CTV reach alongside digital prospecting, device-level CTV reach will appear lower than the true person-level audience, which can make CTV look expensive relative to digital channels that measure per person. You may want to confirm whether co-viewing-adjusted reach is available in the Samba TV report and whether it is the figure used in planning documents.

educate.learn_more_note: Authoritative guidance on Samba TV co-viewing measurement is in the gated Samba TV measurement documentation; contact your Samba TV account team.

investigate.intro: Open the Samba TV report for the campaign in question and confirm which reach metric is being used in planning and reporting summaries.

samba_tv.label: Samba TV
steps: Open the Samba TV reporting interface for the campaign. Locate the reach and impressions columns available in the report view.

Check whether the report offers a co-viewing-adjusted reach column alongside the device-level reach column. The labels may differ by account configuration, but look for any column that references 'persons', 'co-viewing', or 'person-level reach' as distinct from 'devices' or 'households'.
Identify which column the team is using as the primary reach figure in performance summaries, planning decks, or cross-channel reports. Note whether the column label specifies the measurement basis.

If only a device-level reach figure is present in the current report view, check whether a different report template or report export from Samba TV includes co-viewing-adjusted figures, or ask the Samba TV account team which report view surfaces the person-level estimate.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether the Samba TV report being used for planning and post-campaign review includes a co-viewing-adjusted reach figure, and whether that figure is the one cited in cross-channel summaries. A report that includes a person-level co-viewing reach column and uses it as the primary reach figure passes. A report that uses only device-level reach without noting the measurement basis, or that mixes device-level CTV reach with person-level digital reach in a combined summary, is a fail, recorded with the column name found and the report template in use.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record which reach column is used in the Samba TV report and whether it is device-level or co-viewing-adjusted above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Samba TV reporting uses device-level reach figures rather than co-viewing-adjusted person-level reach. Because a single CTV device is often watched by more than one person, device-level counts systematically understate how many people the campaign reached, making CTV appear less efficient than it is on a cost-per-person basis. {{voice_recommend}} confirming which Samba TV report column represents person-level co-viewing reach, using that figure as the primary reach metric in performance summaries, and labeling any cross-channel reach table with the measurement basis for each channel so CTV and digital figures are not compared without context.

audit_checks.title: Co-viewing-adjusted CTV figures are mixed with device-level or cookie-level digital metrics in cross-screen comparisons without noting the difference in measurement basis
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When co-viewing-adjusted CTV reach and device-level or cookie-level digital reach are placed in the same table without labeling the difference, the comparison produces a misleading picture of relative channel performance. Budget decisions made from that comparison may systematically favor or disadvantage one channel based on a reporting artifact rather than actual differences in reach or efficiency.

educate.base:
Cross-screen reporting typically places CTV, linear TV, display, mobile, and social reach figures side by side to show how a campaign distributed its audience across channels. Those figures come from different measurement methodologies, and co-viewing is one of the most consequential differences.

CTV reach measured through Samba TV's co-viewing adjustment is a person-level estimate. It accounts for the viewers in the room, not just the household that owns the device. Digital display or mobile reach figures, by contrast, are typically measured at the device or cookie level, where each impression is attributed to one device and that device is treated as one person. Social platforms use their own logged-in identity graphs, which are also largely person-level but built on different panels and matching logic.

When co-viewing-adjusted CTV reach sits in the same column as device-level display reach and cookie-level social reach without a note, the numbers appear comparable even though they were produced by different methods. A co-viewing-adjusted CTV figure that looks higher than a display reach figure may reflect that the co-viewing adjustment added viewers per household, or it may reflect that CTV genuinely reached more people. There is no way to tell from the combined table alone.

The same issue applies in reverse. If the team uses device-level CTV reach (no co-viewing) alongside person-level digital figures, CTV appears to reach fewer people. If the team uses co-viewing-adjusted CTV alongside device-level digital, CTV appears to reach more. Either way, the cross-channel ranking is driven partly by methodological inconsistency rather than actual audience delivery.

A few signs this check applies:
  • A cross-channel summary table shows CTV reach from Samba TV alongside display, mobile, or social reach without a footnote explaining the measurement basis for each row
  • The team has applied Samba TV's co-viewing adjustment to CTV but is comparing the resulting person-level figure directly against a digital display reach figure that is device-level
  • The cross-channel reach table has a single column labeled 'reach' or 'unique reach' with no distinction by measurement methodology
You may want to add a methodology row or footnote to cross-channel reach tables that specifies whether each channel's reach figure is device-level, cookie-level, or co-viewing-adjusted person-level before using that table to allocate budget across channels.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer comparing CTV and display reach in a cross-channel plan, a co-viewing-adjusted CTV reach figure and a device-level display reach figure are not directly comparable. You may want to confirm that the cross-channel summary used in budget planning notes the measurement basis for each channel's reach figure, so a channel is not favored or penalized because of a methodological difference rather than actual performance.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser using Samba TV alongside paid social and display, mixing a co-viewing-adjusted CTV reach with cookie-level social reach in a combined reach summary can produce a misleading picture of which channel reached more people. You may want to confirm that the performance summary labels the measurement basis for each channel and that any cross-channel deduplication or combined reach figure is built on a consistent person-level methodology.

educate.learn_more_note: Authoritative guidance on Samba TV co-viewing measurement is in the gated Samba TV measurement documentation; contact your Samba TV account team.

investigate.intro: Review the cross-screen or cross-channel reach summary the team uses for planning and post-campaign reporting, and confirm whether the measurement basis for each channel's reach figure is documented.

samba_tv.label: Samba TV
steps: Pull the Samba TV report for the campaign and confirm whether the reach figure exported or cited is the device-level figure or the co-viewing-adjusted person-level figure. Note the column label.
Locate the cross-channel or cross-screen summary used for planning or reporting, whether in a deck, spreadsheet, or reporting dashboard.

For each channel row in that summary, note the measurement methodology: device-level, cookie-level, or co-viewing-adjusted person-level. Pay attention to whether CTV and digital display or social rows carry the same label or different labels.
Check whether any footnote, annotation, or methodology note on the summary table explains the measurement basis for each channel's reach figure. Note whether it is present or absent.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether the cross-channel reach summary in use labels the measurement basis for each channel's reach figure, and whether CTV's co-viewing-adjusted figure is noted as distinct from device-level or cookie-level figures for other channels. A summary that labels the measurement basis per channel, or that applies a consistent person-level methodology across all channels with that noted, passes. A summary that places co-viewing-adjusted CTV reach alongside device-level or cookie-level digital reach in the same unlabeled column is a fail, recorded with the channels present and the labels found on the reach column.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the labels on the reach column in the cross-channel summary and the measurement basis for each channel's figure above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s cross-screen reach summary places Samba TV's co-viewing-adjusted CTV reach alongside device-level or cookie-level digital reach figures in the same unlabeled column. Co-viewing-adjusted reach is a person-level estimate that accounts for viewers in the room beyond the household device, while device-level and cookie-level figures from display and social channels count one impression as one person. Placing these in the same table without noting the difference makes the comparison unreliable as a basis for cross-channel budget decisions. {{voice_recommend}} adding a methodology row or footnote to the cross-channel summary that specifies the measurement basis for each channel's reach figure, and confirming whether any combined or deduplicated reach estimate is built on a consistent person-level methodology before it is used for planning.
